data.title: Polymarket seeks CFTC approval to rejoin the US; Iran may submit adjustment proposal.
data.description:
Headline

▌Polymarket Seeks CFTC Approval to Re-enter the US Market

On April 29, the cryptocurrency prediction market platform Polymarket is seeking support from the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to push for its core exchange to re-enter the US market. This move could bring greater participation from US users to the rapidly growing industry.

▌Iran May Submit Revised Proposal to Pakistan

On April 29, according to US sources, informed sources disclosed that because US President Trump is dissatisfied with Iran's new negotiation proposal, Iran may submit a revised proposal to its mediator, Pakistan, in the coming days. Sources familiar with the mediation process said that Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi will return to Tehran after concluding his visit to Russia on April 28, and is expected to report to the Iranian leadership and hold consultations.

▌The United States prohibits its own individuals or entities from paying Hormuz Strait passage fees to Iran

According to Jinse Finance, on April 29, the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) under the U.S. Treasury Department issued an explanatory document on April 28 stating that the United States prohibits its own individuals or entities from paying Hormuz Strait security passage fees to Iran. The document states that U.S. individuals, U.S. financial institutions, and foreign entities owned or controlled by the United States are prohibited from directly or indirectly paying Hormuz Strait security passage fees to the Iranian government or the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. The OFAC stated that non-U.S. individuals or entities paying Hormuz Strait security passage fees to Iran would also constitute a significant sanction risk.

... Due to US sanctions against the Iranian government and the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps under the pretext of nuclear non-proliferation and counterterrorism, foreign financial institutions and other non-US individuals or entities will face sanctions risks if they conduct certain transactions or activities with sanctioned entities. Transactions involving certain sectors of the Iranian economy may also expose foreign individuals or entities to sanctions risks. On the same day, the Office of Foreign Assets Control also added 17 foreign nationals and 18 foreign entities to its sanctions list related to Iran. (Xinhua) Blockchain Applications ▌US Soldier Denies Using Insider Information to Bet on Maduro's Capture On April 29, a US Army Special Forces sergeant pleaded not guilty to a charge of illegal trading involving classified information about the US operation to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro. Ganon Ken Van Dyke, 38, appeared in Manhattan on Tuesday before US District Judge Margaret Garnett. Prosecutors allege Van Dyke participated in planning and executing the operation to capture Maduro and profited $400,000 by betting on whether the Venezuelan leader would be overthrown on the Polymarket website. Van Dyke was granted bail of $250,000. This case comes amid concerns about insider trading potentially linked to the war with Iran. The White House has warned its staff against trading government information, and Polymarket has been criticized for allowing bets on whether the downed U.S. pilot could be rescued. Customers Bank Partners with OpenAI to Deploy Artificial Intelligence in Commercial Banking Operations On April 28, Customers Bancorp announced that its banking subsidiary, Customers Bank, is fully deploying artificial intelligence across its commercial banking operations through a multi-year strategic collaboration with OpenAI. According to the statement, the collaboration will involve direct on-site involvement of OpenAI's technology team and resources, rather than using off-the-shelf AI tools, to build customized AI capabilities based on the bank's processes, data, and institutional knowledge. The financial terms of the collaboration have not been disclosed. (Dongxin News Agency)

▌ByteDance, Zhipu, and Alibaba Selected as TIME's Top 10 Most Influential AI Companies

On April 28th, TIME magazine released its list of "10 Most Influential Artificial Intelligence Companies of 2026." Unlike simply comparing model capabilities, this list emphasizes the comprehensive shaping power of companies in terms of industry, technological pathways, and social impact. The selected companies are ByteDance, Amazon, Zhipu, OpenAI, Alphabet, Meta, Anthropic, Alibaba, Mistral, and Hugging Face. Among them, three are Chinese companies: ByteDance, Zhipu, and Alibaba.

Cryptocurrency

▌Robinhood's Q1 Crypto Trading Revenue Plunges 47%, Stock Falls 8% in After-Hours Following Weaker-than-Expected Earnings

On April 29th, Robinhood (HOOD) released its Q1 2026 earnings report, showing a significant decline in cryptocurrency trading revenue, with both revenue and earnings per share falling short of market expectations. The report showed that Robinhood's adjusted earnings per share for Q1 were $0.38, lower than the market expectation of $0.39; total revenue increased by 15% year-over-year to $1.07 billion, but fell short of analysts' expectations of $1.14 billion. Cryptocurrency-related revenue decreased by 47% year-over-year to $134 million, lower than $252 million in the same period last year. However, driven by a surge in prediction market contract trading, the company's trading revenue still increased to $623 million. Affected by the earnings report, Robinhood's stock price fell by approximately 8% in after-hours trading.

▌Riot Adjusts $200 Million Coinbase Credit Agreement to Cope with BTC Volatility with Fixed Interest Rate and Buffer Mechanism

On April 28, Nasdaq-listed Bitcoin mining company Riot Platforms announced adjustments to its $200 million Bitcoin collateralized credit agreement with Coinbase to reduce financing cost volatility and mitigate the risk of forced margin calls due to short-term market volatility. The new agreement changes the floating-rate loan, originally linked to the US federal funds rate, to a fixed annual interest rate structure. It also extends the 364-day loan, originally scheduled to mature in April 2026, and allows for an additional year of extension. Furthermore, the new agreement introduces a "two-day rule," meaning that margin calls or deleveraging will only be triggered if the value of the Bitcoin collateral falls below a trigger line for two consecutive days, preventing forced liquidation due to a single-day plunge. In the first quarter of this year, Riot sold 3,778 Bitcoins, raising approximately $289.5 million to cope with pressure on mining profits.

...

▌State Street to Launch Tokenized Fund Service in Luxembourg by Year-End

On April 28th, according to The Block, custody giant State Street plans to launch a tokenized fund service in Luxembourg through its State Street Investment Services by the end of 2026 at the latest, extending its existing fund management, custody, and registration agency functions to the blockchain. The service will operate through its Digital Asset Platform, supporting both tokenized and traditional funds under a unified operating model, and providing infrastructure for the entire lifecycle of issuance, management, and custody. Customers can operate and manage risk through a single interface.

▌BIT-linked whale goes long on ETH again, currently holding 58,000 ETH long positions

On April 28th, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, a BIT-linked whale (which had previously profited over $59 million) opened another long position on ETH. In the past hour, this whale opened a long position of 30,000 ETH with 15x leverage, worth approximately $68 million. Currently, it holds a total of 58,000 ETH long positions through three wallets, with leverage ranging from 15x to 20x, for a total value of approximately $131.82 million.

...

▌Pump.fun Burns $370 Million Worth of PUMP Tokens and Launches Revenue Buyback and Burn Program

On April 29th, Pump.fun announced that it had completed the burning of all previously repurchased PUMP tokens, involving approximately $370 million, representing about 36% of the current circulating supply. In addition, Pump.fun launched a new programmatic buyback and burn mechanism, planning to use 50% of its revenue to buy back and burn PUMP tokens over the next year.

... According to the latest data released on April 28, DeFi United, launched by Aave to address the Kelp DAO theft, has raised over $300 million worth of 132,000 ETH, fully covering the $292 million worth of rsETH lost in the Kelp DAO attack. The main rescuers at present include: 30,765 ETH recovered by Arbitrum DAO; 30,000 ETH provided by Consensys and its founder Joseph Lubin; 30,000 ETH from Mantle; 25,000 ETH from Aave DAO; 5,000 ETH from Aave founder Stani Kulechov; 5,000 ETH from Ether.Fi; 2,500 ETH from Lido; 2,000 ETH from Kelp; 1,000 ETH from the Golem Foundation; 500 ETH from Emilio Frangella, Senior Vice President of Engineering at Aave; 350 ETH from BGD Labs and its co-founder Ernesto; 100 ETH from Karpatkey co-founder Marcelo Ruiz de Olano; and 588 ETH from small donations made by retail traders in the crypto community through tens of thousands of trades.

▌Injective Announces Mainnet Upgrade Proposal Officially Approved, to be Implemented on April 28

On April 28, Injective announced on the X platform that its mainnet upgrade proposal has been officially approved. This upgrade will comprehensively optimize the network's technical performance, enhance the functionality of Injective's on-chain modules, and promote the $INJ token buyback mechanism. The mainnet upgrade will be officially implemented on April 28.

▌Coinbase to Launch VIRTUAL Spot Trading

On April 28, Coinbase will launch Virtuals Protocol (VIRTUAL) spot trading on April 29. If liquidity conditions are met, the VIRTUAL-USD trading pair will open at or after 9:00 AM Pacific Time.

▌Coinbase Adds Gensyn (AI) to Listing Roadmap

On April 29th, according to an official announcement from Coinbase, Coinbase stated that it has added Gensyn (AI) to its asset listing roadmap.

Important Economic Developments

▌Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda: No Need for Immediate Interest Rate Hike

On April 28th, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda stated that there is no need for an immediate interest rate hike. If the current supply shock triggers a secondary cascading effect, then an interest rate hike would be necessary.

(Jinshi) A recent Reuters/Ipsos poll on April 29th showed that President Trump's approval rating has fallen to its lowest level during his term as Americans become increasingly dissatisfied with his handling of the cost of living and the unpopular war in Iraq. The poll showed that only 34% of Americans approve of Trump's performance, down from 36% in a poll conducted April 15-20. Regarding his handling of the cost of living, only 22% of respondents approved, down from 25% previously. Trump's popularity has been declining since he took office in January 2025, when 47% of Americans still approved of his performance.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged the following morning is 100%

According to CME's "FedWatch" on April 29th: the probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 100%. The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 2.6%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 97.4%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks.

... Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Market Outlook for April 2026: A Mixed Recovery
data.description:
Author: Tanay Ved, coinmetrics Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • The cryptocurrency market continued its recovery momentum in April, with Bitcoin (BTC) rising approximately 16% to over $78,000.

  • April saw the strongest monthly net inflow into spot Bitcoin ETFs since October 2025, while futures positions were short, with most funds in negative territory.

  • Tokenized government bonds are becoming a core layer for on-chain yield and reserves, competing with idle stablecoins and high-risk crypto collateral by providing short-term exposure to US government debt.

  • Tokenized government bonds are becoming a core layer for on-chain yield and reserves, competing with idle stablecoins and high-risk crypto collateral by providing short-term exposure to US government debt.

  • This article will outline the key developments that have impacted the cryptocurrency market over the past month. Market Overview In April, the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies rose by approximately 10% to around $2.7 trillion, continuing its recovery momentum amid ongoing geopolitical conflicts and volatility in the oil and energy markets. Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH) rose by 16% and 14% respectively, and major stock indices also rebounded from their March lows. Bitcoin's market share remains around 57% and is moving towards the upper end of its recent price range. Other notable cryptocurrencies that month included Zcash (+59%) and Morpho (+33%).

    Source: CM Reference Interest Rate

    Source: CM Network Data Pro

    Data Source: CM Market Data Feed

    Source: CM Market Data Pro

    Source: CM Network Data Pro

    Preview
Gain a broader understanding of the crypto industry through informative reports, and engage in in-depth discussions with other like-minded authors and readers. You are welcome to join us in our growing Coinlive community:https://t.me/CoinliveSG

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | China Halts Manus Acquisition
data.description:
DeFi Data

1. Total Market Cap of DeFi Tokens: $54.527 Billion

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $43.88

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $830.33 billion$100 million

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $8.044 billion

2.24 hours NFT transaction volume 1.964 billion USD

Toutiao

China Halts Manus Acquisition, Meta Responds

On April 28, Chinese authorities halted the acquisition of artificial intelligence startup Manus by US tech giant Meta, demanding the company withdraw from the acquisition. On April 27, the National Development and Reform Commission of China announced on its official website that the Office of the Foreign Investment Security Review Mechanism had "made a decision prohibiting foreign investment in the Manus project in accordance with laws and regulations, and demanded that the parties withdraw the acquisition transaction." Meta issued a statement on the same day stating that the transaction complied with applicable laws and expressed its expectation that the issues raised in the Chinese investigation would be properly resolved, but did not provide further details. Meta announced its $2 billion acquisition of Manus at the end of December last year, and China confirmed its involvement in the investigation in January this year.

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Gemini Launches File Generation Capability: Supports Direct Creation of PDF, CSV, ZIP, and Python Sandbox

    On April 28th, according to Nokiapoweruser, the Google Gemini app has added file generation capabilities. Users can directly create various formats such as TXT, Markdown, PDF, CSV, and configuration files within conversations, and can also package multiple files into a ZIP for download at once. Google has not officially announced this; users discovered it through testing and it spread on social media.

    2. Bitcoin Mining Machine Core Scientific Transforms into Artificial Intelligence, Advancing 1.5GW Data Center Project

    On April 28th, Bitcoin miner Core Scientific plans to expand its Texas operations into a large artificial intelligence data center campus with a total generating capacity of up to 1.5 gigawatts.

    The company announced Monday that it is transforming its Pecos, Texas data center into a high-density hosting center to support artificial intelligence workloads and meet the growing demand for computing infrastructure. Approximately 1 gigawatt of the planned capacity is expected to be available for lease. Core Scientific CEO Adam Sullivan stated, “We will continue to leverage our deep in-house expertise to differentiate how we build and scale next-generation AI infrastructure.” 3. Customers Bank Partners with OpenAI to Deploy AI in Commercial Banking Operations On April 28, Customers Bancorp announced that its banking subsidiary, Customers Bank, is fully deploying artificial intelligence across its commercial banking operations through a multi-year strategic collaboration with OpenAI. According to the statement, the collaboration will involve direct on-site involvement of OpenAI's technology team and resources, rather than using off-the-shelf AI tools, to build custom AI capabilities based on the bank's processes, data, and institutional knowledge. The financial terms of the collaboration were not disclosed. 4. Accenture to roll out Microsoft Copilot 365 AI assistant to approximately 743,000 employees. On April 28, Accenture announced it will roll out Microsoft's Copilot 365 AI assistant to all of its approximately 743,000 employees, marking the chatbot's largest enterprise order to date. The two companies did not disclose financial details of the agreement in a joint statement. This is a significant boost for Microsoft, as only slightly more than 3% of its over 450 million 365 enterprise users pay for the $30 per month service. 5. ByteDance, Zhipu, and Alibaba named among Time's 10 most influential AI companies. On April 28, Time magazine released its list of "10 Most Influential Artificial Intelligence Companies for 2026." Unlike simply comparing model capabilities, this list emphasizes the comprehensive shaping power of industry, technological pathways, and social impact. The selected companies include ByteDance, Amazon, Zhipu, OpenAI, Alphabet, Meta, Anthropic, Alibaba, Mistral, and Hugging Face. Among them, three are Chinese companies: ByteDance, Zhipu, and Alibaba.

    DeFi Hot Topics

    1. Bitmine just pledged $214 million worth of ETH

    On April 28th, according to Arkham monitoring, Bitmine just pledged $214 million worth of ETH; Bitmine currently holds a total of $8.45 billion worth of pledged ETH, accounting for 9.5% of all pledged ETH on the network.

    ...

    2. Messari's In-Depth Analysis of Bitget's Four-Layer AI Architecture: Pioneering a Comprehensive AI Calling System

    On April 28th, Messari's latest Pulse report provided an in-depth analysis of the early implementation progress of Bitget's AI trading system. The report points out that Bitget has built a four-layer AI product architecture covering GetAgent (market analysis), GetClaw (autonomous execution), Agent Hub (developer access), and Gracy AI (strategy interaction). Data shows that Gracy AI attracted over 460,000 users in just 11 days after its launch, generating over 2.6 million replies and achieving 390 million impressions; GetAgent has surpassed 450,000 registered users, achieving over 100 million impressions during its early invitation testing phase and accumulating over 25,000 potential users.

    ... Messari highlighted Agent Hub's infrastructure capabilities in its report, noting that Bitget is currently the only trading platform that simultaneously supports a complete call system for MCP Server, REST/WebSocket API, Skills, and CLI. Agent Hub currently integrates 5 analytical AI Skills and 15+ data tools, comprehensively covering macro, technical, sentiment, and intelligence analysis. The execution layer, GetClaw, constructs a risk control defense through sub-account segregation, sandbox exercises, and fund limits. It is currently integrated with Telegram and will be expanded to Discord and the App in the future.

    3. LayerZero-linked wallet deposits 1 million ZRO tokens, worth $1.43 million, into Binance

    On April 28th, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, a wallet associated with the LayerZero team deposited 1 million ZRO tokens, worth $1.43 million, into Binance.

    The wallet still holds 29 million ZRO tokens, worth $41.34 million, and may continue to deposit more.

    4. Layer 1 project Monad's official X account frozen

    On April 28, according to the X platform, Monad's official X account has been frozen, and the reason for the freeze is currently unconfirmed.

    5. Morpho CEO: Institutional entry into DeFi is inevitable; control and compliance are core demands

    On April 27, Morpho CEO Paul Frambot posted on X that he had communicated with several large institutions over the past week to understand their views on the current DeFi situation, and the core conclusions are as follows: First, institutional interest will not disappear. The reason is simple: distribution channels will not disappear—massive assets under management (AUM), payments, and lending businesses are going on-chain.

    ... Almost all fintech companies aspire to full on-chain functionality. For institutions, this isn't a question of "whether or not," but rather "a must." Secondly, they have lost trust in the pool/hub model; institutions and distributors want complete control, including control over the code, risk, and compliance. Simultaneously, they desire flexibility—the ability to isolate their operations and connect to compatible global liquidity networks. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, provides articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Nvidia hits new highs but still underperforms: Why is Wall Street even more bullish?
data.description: Nvidia (NVDA.O) is once again in the spotlight on Wall Street due to the expanding demand for AI computing power and a potential shift in capital return strategies. Despite hitting its first all-time high since October last year on Monday, its year-to-date gains have significantly lagged behind the overall semiconductor sector, a gap that has ironically become a key reason for some institutions' bullish outlook. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rose more than 36% in April and is nearly 50% above its 200-day moving average. Goldman Sachs' trading division notes that this level of divergence is rare since the dot-com bubble. In contrast, Nvidia's gains over the same period were just over 20%. Extending the timeframe to 2026 to the present, this gap widens further: Nvidia is up about 15% this year, while the index as a whole has risen nearly 46%. Trivariate Research noted in its report that Nvidia ranked only 49th among all semiconductor and equipment stocks over the past three months, and even with the recent significant rebound, its relative performance remains low. This structural gap has led the market to reassess Nvidia's valuation and strategic potential. Bank of America analyst Vivek Arya believes that as ecosystem investments are completed, the company is in a position to reduce capital expenditure intensity and shift its focus to shareholder returns, including increasing dividends and expanding share buybacks. This shift could attract more yield-oriented capital while alleviating market concerns about mergers and acquisitions and supply chain financing, thereby driving valuation recovery. Currently, Nvidia's shareholder returns are significantly low. The company's quarterly dividend is only 1 cent per share, corresponding to a dividend yield of approximately 0.02%, far below the peer average of 0.89%. Bank of America estimates that raising the dividend yield to 0.5% to 1% would bring it close to Apple's (AAPL.O) approximately 0.4% and Microsoft's (MSFT.O) approximately 0.8%. Achieving this goal would require approximately $26 billion to $51 billion, representing 15% to 30% of 2026 free cash flow, while still allowing for share buybacks and ecosystem investments. Trivariate's Adam Parker, focusing on long-term market capitalization potential, believes Nvidia has the potential to reach a $10 trillion market capitalization by 2030. He points out that the company is "essentially more of an industry than a single enterprise," thus creating a new buying logic when investors consider taking profits in the short term. Demand-side support is also strengthening. JPMorgan analyst Harlan Sur predicts that AI-related demand will drive multi-year growth in Nvidia's data center GPU business. Meanwhile, short-term industry trends are more driven by CPU manufacturers. Intel (INTC.O) saw its first-quarter earnings significantly exceed expectations, sending its stock price up nearly 24% in a single day, with AMD (AMD.O) also benefiting. On the supply side, the early locking in of computing power demand is increasing industry visibility. JPMorgan points out that customers are locking in capacity in advance to cope with future computing demand growth, giving Nvidia and Broadcom (AVGO.O) longer-term order backlogs until fiscal year 2027. Companies have also disclosed that demand visibility for their Blackwell and Vera Rubin architecture products has exceeded $1 trillion. While the medium- to long-term logic is clear, short-term trends still face key variables. This week, major cloud computing customers including Amazon (AMZN.O), Meta (META.O), Microsoft (MSFT.O), and Alphabet (GOOGL.O) will release their earnings reports. Their capital expenditures and AI investment pace may directly influence market perceptions of Nvidia's growth path. Wedbush's team, led by Dan Ives, predicts that the market will continue to focus on AI monetization capabilities and capital expenditure trends ahead of the major tech companies' earnings releases, and the overall performance this week is expected to remain strong. Nvidia itself is expected to release its next earnings report on May 20th. Institutional opinions are generally bullish. Visible Alpha statistics show that among 13 analysts they track, 12 have given a "buy" rating, with an average target price of approximately $268, representing about 24% upside potential from current levels.

data.title: OpenAI's backstabbing of Microsoft for hundreds of billions in ransom
data.description:
After painstakingly sponsoring his girlfriend's university education, she immediately broke up with him after graduation, citing a desire for freedom…

How would that feel?

Microsoft knows.

Yesterday, OpenAI and Microsoft, after a seven-year legal battle, jointly announced: a contract revision!

OpenAI has finally gained the freedom it longed for.

The price is that it must repay the sponsorship it received tenfold.

The Root of the Contradiction

Let's rewind to 2019.

At that time, OpenAI was still an ideal laboratory that relied on donations to survive, and it was even struggling to sustain the training of GPT-3.

Microsoft's situation wasn't much better. Its cloud business, Azure, couldn't compete with AWS, and its search business, Bing, was being crushed by Google. It desperately needed a technological breakthrough to turn things around. In July of that year, Microsoft officially invested $1 billion in OpenAI, marking the beginning of their first collaboration: Microsoft provided OpenAI with Azure computing power, while OpenAI used GPT technology to help Microsoft build products like GitHub Copilot. From the outside, Microsoft was seen as providing not only money but also computing power, practically a savior for OpenAI. However, for Microsoft, like many other venture capital investments, this was likely just an experiment, with no real expectation of significant returns. Therefore, Microsoft even agreed to some rather outrageous conditions. According to details of the agreement revealed by The Information, one clause stipulated that when OpenAI's non-profit board determined that AI had evolved to AGI, it had the right to immediately cut off Microsoft's access to the technology. This is the well-known "AGI clause." Looking back now, it seems Microsoft's legal team at the time was out of their minds to agree to such a condition. Seven years ago, the so-called grand model was still very rudimentary, and AGI was nothing more than a fantasy from science fiction. Even if it could be realized, it would be a very distant prospect. It was a pie-in-the-sky promise, its main purpose at the time being hype. No one could have predicted that just three years later, ChatGPT's sudden evolution at the end of 2022 would completely alter the balance of power. OpenAI was no longer just one of Microsoft's dispensable underlings; it suddenly possessed the power to sway the balance. Cracks naturally appeared. Over the past few years, while OpenAI has brought huge profits to Microsoft, Altman has been relentlessly proclaiming on social media, "We're not far from AGI," and "AGI could be achieved at any time." Showcasing technological progress was secondary; the more important thing was to demonstrate its own "independence." Microsoft was naturally very unhappy with this behavior. Because of these inexplicable clauses, what could be the most successful venture capital investment in human history has become a ticking time bomb. What exactly constitutes AGI? According to leaked documents from 2024: AGI would be achieved once OpenAI develops an AI system that generates a total profit of $100 billion for its early investors (including Microsoft). By October 2025, with OpenAI completing its for-profit restructuring, things became even more concrete. Following the restructuring, Microsoft acquired approximately 27% of OpenAI's equity, valued at around $135 billion at the time. When an investment balloons to the hundreds of billions of dollars, the time has come to defuse this ticking time bomb called AGI. The Cost of Freedom After several weeks of closed-door talks, the two sides finally eliminated legal risks and yesterday produced a completely new agreement. Its core content can be summarized in one sentence: stripping away the technology bet, locking in long-term cash flow, and restructuring distribution channels. 1. Abolish the AGI clause. Previously, the contract allowed OpenAI to dictate AGI; in reality, Microsoft wouldn't allow OpenAI to do so. "Don't tell me what you think, tell me what I think." Whether AGI is realized must be verified by an "independent expert panel" to be considered valid. However, by the time these experts have figured out what AGI is, Musk's rockets might already be on Mars, which is meaningless for OpenAI, which is eager to commercialize. This effectively abolishes the so-called AGI clause. Given this, it would be better for both sides to compromise and decouple the change of rights from the vague "technical milestones," directly replacing it with a simple and straightforward "fixed date." Microsoft retains a non-exclusive intellectual property license for OpenAI models and products, extending its validity to 2032.

For research-level intellectual property, Microsoft's rights will last until the expert panel verifies AGI or until 2030 (whichever comes first).

2. Microsoft will no longer pay; OpenAI will contribute unilaterally.

In the old agreement, both parties charged each other.

Microsoft sells OpenAI's services on Azure, giving OpenAI 20% of its profits; OpenAI, in turn, gives Microsoft 20% of its total revenue. However, under a new agreement, Microsoft will no longer pay OpenAI its share of Azure resale revenue, keeping it all for itself. What about OpenAI? OpenAI will still need to continue paying Microsoft the same share of revenue, this "protection fee" until 2030. At the same time, OpenAI has also committed to purchasing $250 billion worth of Azure cloud services from Microsoft. This is clearly an "unequal treaty." Why would Ultraman agree to this? Did he also drink fake alcohol? The main reason is the next point. 3. Giving up "exclusive sales rights." OpenAI can now sell all its products on any cloud service provider's platform. While Microsoft retains the title of "premier cloud partner" and new products will debut on Azure first, this is merely a facade. The agreement is cleverly worded: unless Microsoft is unable or chooses not to support the required capabilities, OpenAI can find someone else. Therefore, immediately after the official announcement yesterday, Amazon's CEO posted on Amazon Web Services (AWS) that AWS will make all of OpenAI's models available to customers through the Bedrock service "in the coming weeks." Clearly, this is a major event in the B2B market. Enterprise customers hate being "locked in." OpenAI has enabled multi-cloud deployment, allowing B2B customers, especially large government projects requiring extremely high data security and isolation, to choose where to deploy their models. In short, for OpenAI, the new protocol is generally more beneficial than harmful. Although burdened with a heavy financial load, requiring continuous payments to Microsoft for the next five years, OpenAI's commercialization channels are more flexible. Based on AWS's enterprise customer base, the number of OpenAI's enterprise customers is expected to grow by 50% by the end of 2026, from the current 120,000 to 180,000 after partnering with AWS. More importantly, investors no longer need to worry about Microsoft holding them accountable after achieving AGI, nor about AGI being a distant prospect and investment returns not materializing. This eliminates the biggest uncertainty surrounding the IPO. And what about Microsoft? Microsoft has also shed many unnecessary burdens. The agreement to rewrite the agreement this time wasn't solely due to the existence of the AGI clause. Firstly, there was external pressure. Amazon AWS had long coveted Microsoft's monopoly in AI cloud services. In February of this year, Amazon launched a fierce offensive against OpenAI, pledging up to $50 billion in investment and planning to expand the existing $38 billion AWS agreement to $100 billion. Simultaneously, AWS would become the "exclusive third-party cloud distributor" for OpenAI's Frontier enterprise platform, launched in early April. This clearly infringed on Microsoft's exclusive rights, and Microsoft even considered suing OpenAI for breach of contract. However, in the past, litigation in such matters would likely result in a lose-lose situation, so it was better for both parties to sit down and settle privately. Of course, what's even more important is computing power! Computing power! Computing power! Training large models is, frankly, burning money. In OpenAI's eyes, although Microsoft has restricted its freedom, its core value as a "computing power ATM" cannot be denied. Microsoft's frantic land acquisitions and data center buildouts, along with its scramble for power quotas worldwide, still haven't satisfied OpenAI's insatiable appetite—it's a bottomless pit. Moreover, OpenAI's loyalty is questionable, making the money a complete waste. Given this, it would be better to let OpenAI find customers on other cloud providers. This would not only alleviate the infrastructure pressure on Microsoft Azure but also allow OpenAI to find more willing investors to share its infrastructure costs. At least until the contract expires, regardless of how much money OpenAI makes on AWS, as long as OpenAI's valuation increases, Microsoft's investment returns will rise accordingly. This also ensures that Microsoft's core product lines, such as Office Copilot and Windows OS, won't face a crisis of unreliable technical supply. What's wrong with making money passively? As for what happened later, while OpenAI was busy flirting with AWS, Microsoft was also secretly pursuing a deep collaboration with Anthropic, even using the Claude model to drive some of its own agent products. These two companies had long been pursuing their own agendas, each acting as a backup plan for the other. The current outcome is relatively positive. This incident is not merely about the on-again, off-again relationship between two giants. Once, AGI was revered as a silicon-based god, even enshrined in contracts as sacred dogma. But now, its most devout followers have personally stripped it of its "sacredness." It has become something that can be defined by an "independent expert panel," a combination of financial caps, intellectual property years (2032), and revenue-sharing deadlines (2030), a commodity that requires multi-channel distribution to earn meager profits… The entire AI industry, after three years of rapid development, has shed its early idealistic aura and entered the deep waters of commercialization. And yesterday, it officially drew a comma full of realism.

data.title: A single ban wiped out $2 billion.
data.description:
On April 27, 2026, the Office of the Foreign Investment Security Review Mechanism (National Development and Reform Commission) issued a decision prohibiting foreign investment in the Manus project, requiring the parties involved to withdraw the acquisition. These few dozen words effectively ended the deal, which was valued at over $2 billion. Manus's years of product development, legal framework construction, financing, and exit strategies all collapsed and went down the drain. This is the first publicly halted foreign acquisition in the AI ​​field since the "Measures for Foreign Investment Security Review" came into effect in January 2021. This transaction has a unique aspect: both parties are legally offshore: Meta is a US company, and Manus has completed its relocation to Singapore and established a holding structure in the Cayman Islands. However, Chinese regulators ultimately decided to prohibit the investment. The spillover effects of this case, along with AI companies like Moonlight, ByteDance, and Leapfrog, are facing clearer compliance guidance. Behind this lies a deeper issue: traditional offshore structure practices are becoming completely ineffective. Entrepreneurs need to clearly define their compliance path from Day 0. This article doesn't tell stories, it delivers practical information - what laws and regulations govern this; where is the red line drawn for "bathing-style" overseas expansion; and how companies should choose their strategies starting today.

According to laws and regulations

Looking back at the Manus case, the initial discussions within the industry mostly focused on "what happened" - migration, separation, and injunction. But as the details of the case gradually surfaced, the legal community's focus returned to a more fundamental question: On what grounds could regulators halt this transaction? What law? What regulation?

The answer is not in any single law, but rather a three-tiered, progressive regulatory logic. These three layers work together to ultimately form an inescapable review logic.

The answer is not in any single law, but rather in a three-tiered, progressive regulatory logic. These three layers combine to form an unavoidable review logic.

Reviewing the Manus case, the initial discussions within the industry mostly focused on "what happened" - migration, separation, and injunction. But as the details of the case gradually surfaced, the legal community's focus returned to a more fundamental question: On what grounds could regulators halt this transaction? What law? What regulation?

The answer is not in any single law, but in a three-tiered, progressive regulatory logic. These three layers work together to form an unavoidable review logic.

The answer is not in any single law, but rather in a three-tiered, progressive regulatory logic.

First layer: Identifying "Chinese entities" - the underlying basis for penetrating review

This is the legal starting point of the entire case: What kind of company is Manus?

From a legal perspective, the answer seems clear - Manus has completed its relocation to Singapore, its holding structure is located in the Cayman Islands, and its parent company, Butterfly Effect Pte, is a genuine Singaporean entity.

From a legal perspective, the answer seems clear - Manus has completed its relocation to Singapore, its holding structure is located in the Cayman Islands, and its parent company, Butterfly Effect Pte, is a genuine Singaporean entity.

This is also the core legal argument of the Manus team throughout the entire transaction: "Our main structure has been converted to an offshore structure." However, the regulator's response was: Form doesn't matter; substance matters. Jingtian & Gongcheng Law Firm systematically analyzed from a legal perspective why the "offshore legal shell" failed in the Manus case. The root cause lies in the fact that core AI assets have an unbreakable and substantial connection with Chinese legal jurisdiction in four dimensions: Team Dimension: The engineering team that masters the underlying core logic has accumulated R&D experience domestically for a long time, and its technical capabilities were trained and developed within China; Computing Power Dimension: Domestic R&D has created a path dependency in technical interfaces and computing power scheduling, and the core system's architectural genes are marked with a Chinese label; Algorithm Dimension: The core model weights were developed and trained within China, representing the most legally significant "technological source." Data Dimension: The training data accumulated from Human Feedback Reinforcement Learning (RLHF) based on massive user interactions is highly concentrated within China. These four dimensions point to the same conclusion: Manus's legal form is Singaporean, but Manus's "technological substance" as a company—its source, core, and foundation—is entirely within China. Based on the principle of "substance over form," from a regulatory perspective, this substantive connection is sufficient to constitute the basis for penetrating scrutiny—the first cornerstone of all subsequent legal actions. So, although Xiao Hong founded Butterfly Effect Technology in Beijing in 2022, established a "Cayman Islands-Hong Kong-Beijing" red-chip structure in 2023, and relocated the company to Singapore in 2025, completing the team separation and business isolation, the law does not consider "when the relocation occurred," but rather "where it originated." Any technology assets originating in China do not change their nationality due to a mere registration change.

Second Layer: Export Restrictions and Regulatory Avoidance - The Legal Characterization of "Bath-Style" Overseas Expansion

Once the first layer is established: Manus is essentially recognized as a "domestic enterprise," the legal logic of the second layer follows: transferring core assets overseas is itself an export activity. Export activities are subject to export control regulations.

Once the first layer is established: Manus is deemed to be a "domestic enterprise," the legal logic of the second layer immediately follows: transferring core assets overseas is itself an export activity. Export activities are subject to export control regulations.

Manus's three-step approach, in the eyes of regulators, forms a complete puzzle of "export control circumvention": Step 1: Relocation of the main entity. The company moved its main entity from China to Singapore, establishing an offshore entity, Butterfly Effect Pte, and setting up a Cayman Islands holding structure. Legally, this completed the first step of "de-Sinicization." Step 2: Relocation of the team and assets. Nearly two-thirds of its employees in China were laid off in a swift move (80 out of 120), with only about 40 core technical personnel remaining, who were relocated to Singapore. The third step is to separate data from business operations. This involves clearing domestic social media accounts, blocking access from Chinese IP addresses, and terminating cooperation with local partners such as Alibaba's Tongyi Qianwen. Legally, the export of core technical personnel's technical knowledge, R&D capabilities, and algorithmic experience constitutes "technology export" under the "Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export." Furthermore, according to the "Data Security Law" and the "Measures for Security Assessment of Data Export," the large amount of user interaction data trained before the separation was highly concentrated in China – the data's origin was already written into the model, making the separation process untraceable and impossible to delete. Therefore, the logic of regulatory penetration can be summarized in a cold, hard statement: The code is written on Chinese soil, the data resides with Chinese users – this is "Chinese assets," and transferring them is exporting them; exports are subject to regulation. The essence of "shower-style overseas expansion" is using formal compliance to conceal substantive violations; this is a systematic circumvention of export control regulations. The third layer: Proactive reporting mechanism - You can't say "I don't know". If the first two layers are "substantive violations", the third layer is a "procedural violation" - and it's the easiest to be convicted of. Article 4 of the "Measures for the Security Review of Foreign Investment" clearly stipulates that for foreign investments involving important information technology, key technologies, and other fields, the parties involved "shall proactively report to the working mechanism office before implementing the investment." This is a mandatory prior reporting obligation, not a "recommendation" or a "supplementary report after an incident". Throughout the entire transaction process, until the completion of the settlement, Manus and Meta never made any voluntary declarations to Chinese regulatory authorities. During the months-long settlement period, Manus and its investors seemed to have reached a dangerous tacit agreement: they wouldn't proactively open the window unless the regulators knocked. In legal practice, "failure to report when required" is itself a serious violation. It sends the signal that either the violation was knowingly committed or intentionally evaded regulations. In either case, regulators will not let it go unpunished. A compliance lawyer summarized the situation after the Manus case: "The biggest compliance flaw exposed by the Manus case wasn't the controversial applicability of a particular regulation, but rather that the company completely abandoned its reporting obligations to Chinese regulators. In the legal system, evading procedures is more intolerable to regulators than substantive violations." Looking back, Manus's fate was actually predetermined at the first level: once a thorough review determined that you were a "de facto Chinese entity," the second level of export control logic and the third level of reporting obligations automatically unlocked. These three layers of legal principles are progressive and interconnected, forming a logical closed loop. Within this loop, no link allows for any room for "luck." Why the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC)? The Ministry of Commerce acted first. On January 8, 2026, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Commerce publicly stated that it would conduct an assessment and investigation into the acquisition's consistency with relevant laws and regulations concerning export controls, technology import and export, and foreign investment. However, on April 27, it was the NDRC that made the final decision. There's more to this departmental switch than meets the eye. Some experts believe that the Ministry of Commerce is basing its decisions on the "Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export," which provides very specific descriptions of controlled technologies: artificial intelligence interactive interface technology specifically designed for Chinese and minority languages. However, after its restructuring, Manus has switched all its services to English, excluding Chinese users. This means that simply following the export control route might lead to some controversy. This is the space for debate regarding the applicability of regulations. But we tend to believe in a deeper meaning, since the applicability of law is less important than political considerations in terms of prioritization. The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) manages "security reviews," while the Ministry of Commerce manages "technology import and export." The NDRC's involvement signifies that this matter has shifted from a "business" issue to a matter of "sovereignty." In other words, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC), as a macro-level department with more comprehensive economic management power than the Ministry of Commerce, sends a clear signal through its intervention – this is not an accidental enforcement action targeting a particular company, but a systemic deterrent aimed at preventing future repercussions. Killing one is to warn a hundred. All those practitioners who were still observing now see where the red line is drawn – not in the ambiguity of a specific clause, but on the indisputable ultimate standard of safeguarding national security. Based on the Manus case and the "penetrating review" principle established in the "Measures for the Security Review of Foreign Investment," the following four red lines are clear. If any of these lines are crossed, the "shower-style" overseas expansion strategy is no longer viable. Red Line 1: Founder Holds Chinese Passport and Has Not Revoked Chinese Citizenship Manus founder Xiao Hong is a Chinese citizen. China's export control law covers natural persons. This means the founder himself may also become a target of regulatory scrutiny, and related arrangements cannot be understood solely at the company level. A more severe reality lies across the Pacific: In North American VC geopolitical risk assessments, the financing environment for Chinese founders is also tightening. Under geopolitical pressure, leading Silicon Valley venture capital firms like a16z have drastically reduced their investment interest in founders holding Chinese passports. Manus's Series B funding round was led by Benchmark, but Benchmark subsequently faced strong backlash from the US political establishment, with several Republican senators calling the deal "aiding the Chinese government." Investors at Silicon Valley's Founders Fund were blunt: "The founder is Chinese, the company is in Beijing, and the core technology is a general-purpose AI agent – ​​that's the 'original sin.'" Both sides are closing in. If you have a Chinese passport, American capital is uneasy; if you have Chinese technology, Chinese regulators won't let go. This gap is much narrower than most people imagine. Red Line Two: Receiving Money from State-Owned Assets

Red Line Two: Receiving Money from State-Owned Assets

It's not only "direct investment by state-owned funds" that counts as state-owned assets. Government-guided funds at all levels, state-owned components in RMB fund LPs, and policy bank loans—these all fall within the scope of "state-owned asset infusion." And those small, seemingly insignificant subsidies like office space, computing power, and talent allowances—complained about during the application process—will all be noted down when it comes to accountability.

Red Line Three: The First Line of Code Was Written in China The initial location where the core code was written, the location where the algorithm model was trained, and the storage location of the technical documentation—these seemingly "purely technical" facts all constitute legal proof of the "source of technology." Manus's early development was completed in China, and when the team moved to Singapore, the code they took with them already constituted a technology export. Manus never made any technology export declaration for this transfer. Red Line Four: Use of Chinese Data This is a common illusion among many AI entrepreneurs: they believe that as long as they clear out domestic users and block Chinese IPs later, their company will be clean. However, in the eyes of regulators, 'technical substance' is not only about code, but also about data fundamentals. The Data Security Law and the Measures for Security Assessment of Cross-border Data Transfer have clear review requirements for cross-border transfers involving "important data". Although Manus shut down its Chinese service and blocked Chinese IPs, the user interaction data accumulated in its early stages had already completed the core training of its model – the data's DNA was etched into the model's weights and could not be recovered or deleted through "post-processing." Since the data originated from Chinese users, the model carries a Chinese label.

Entrepreneurs in Specific Industries: Choose Sides, Start Now

The "Security Review Measures" establish a security review mechanism for foreign investment that may affect national security, focusing on defense security fields such as the military industry, as well as important areas where foreign capital gains actual control, such as important information technology, key technologies, major infrastructure, and important resources.

According to laws and regulations was a principle in the past, and it will be an ironclad rule from now on.

Today's focus is not on convicting anyone, but on recognizing a trend: the gray areas that used to be exploited by changing the place of registration, structure, and entity are being continuously compressed. For founders, going global is no longer a game of "circumventing regulations first, then fulfilling compliance later," but rather a game where they need to figure out their entity, funding, technology, data, and application path from Day 0.

We hope that every founding team seeking a way out in the cracks of this era, whether you choose to go all out on the US-funded track or cultivate the domestic system in depth, can understand the rules, gain a foothold, and go further.

We hope that every founding team seeking a way out in the cracks of this era, whether you choose to go all out on the US-funded track or choose to cultivate the domestic system in depth, can understand the rules, gain a foothold, and go further.

data.title: How to identify whether a crypto project is a scam?
data.description: Content Summary and Introduction:Don't just look at the white paper, team background, or audit results. Instead, check the smart contracts for risks such as hidden administrator privileges, arbitrary issuance, and single points of control. Also, pay attention to whether token distribution is excessively concentrated and whether there are transparent lock-up and governance mechanisms. Even if the project itself is reliable, the trading execution environment can still pose risks. It is recommended to use an intent-driven matching architecture to reduce MEV, front-running, and slippage losses.

data.title: How ordinary people can properly participate in tokenized pre-IPO
data.description:
TL;DR

In Q1 2026, weekly trading volume of commodity perpetual contracts (gold, silver, crude oil) on cryptocurrency exchanges surged from $38.1M to $25B, an increase of 65,463%. Tokenization of traditional assets will be the main theme of Crypto over the next 5-10 years, and pre-IPO tokenization is just the latest category to join this wave.

In April, three leading exchanges—Bitget, Gate, and Binance (PreStocks)—almost simultaneously launched SpaceX-related tokenized products. While their compliance methods differ, their essence is the same: breaking down the pre-IPO market share, previously only available to ultra-high-net-worth clients, and selling it to retail investors.

This article mainly clarifies two things: first, what exactly is traditional Pre-IPO, and second, how retail investors can participate. Traditional asset tokenization will be the main theme of Crypto for the next 5-10 years. According to statistics, in Q1 2026, the weekly trading volume of commodity perpetual contracts (gold, silver, and crude oil) on cryptocurrency exchanges surged from $38.1 million to $25 billion, an increase of 65,463%. After Binance launched its TradeFi Perpetual section in January, its cumulative trading volume exceeded $153 billion and the number of transactions exceeded 114 million in three months; its XAG (silver) contract had an average daily trading volume of $1.31 billion, and its global market share surged from 0.2% to 4.9% (a 23.5-fold increase). The most striking example was the Iran-Iraq War at the end of February. The US and Israel's strikes against Iran occurred over the weekend, and traditional futures, stocks, and forex markets were all closed. Only the cryptocurrency market remained open globally. At that time, Hyperliquid's perpetual crude oil surged by 5%, Tether's XAUT gold saw a single-day trading volume exceeding $300 million, and Bitwise's CIO called it "the weekend that changed finance." US stocks, precious metals, crude oil, and forex—assets that were previously traded only on weekdays from 9 am to 4 pm—are being tokenized, on-chained, and provided with 24/7 global liquidity. Pre-IPO tokenization is just the latest category to join this wave.

What exactly is Pre-IPO?

The Pre-IPO secondary market (trading of existing shares) has existed for over a decade, with global trading volume reaching $160 billion in 2024, and the US direct secondary market alone accounting for $61.1 billion. Buyers are primarily private offices, sovereign wealth funds, institutional investors, and high-net-worth individuals. Individual transactions typically exceed $10 million, effectively excluding retail investors.

Sources: Caplight PitchBook, Augment

After a US company's IPO, there is a standard lock-up period: SEC Rule 144 plus the underwriters' agreement stipulates that early shareholders and employees' shares cannot be sold on the public market for 6 months after the company's IPO. This rule applies to almost all US companies (Facebook, Coinbase, Reddit, Cerebras all have a 6-month period). This is why Bitget/Gate's pre-IPO "tokens will have to wait 6 months to be redeemed," but it does not affect pre-market trading.

Sharing Real Transaction Details of Pre-IPO

Extremely High Ticket Size Threshold

Traditional pre-IPO ticket sizes generally start at $10M, and almost no one takes orders below $1M—not because they don't want to, but because the fixed costs per transaction (legal fees, KYC, SPV setup, channel fees) are prohibitive. Therefore, this move by exchanges is a disruptive attempt, breaking down class barriers. In the past, retail investors (and only advanced players with US stock accounts, etc.) could only participate in trading after the IPO. Now, although exchanges are a bit more expensive, at least they have given ordinary people the opportunity to participate. Broker/FA Chaos

A cross-border pre-IPO deal typically goes through multiple layers: bottom-level GP - Rep (seller representative) - first-level broker - second-level broker - ... - FA - client

Each layer adds a 1-5% fee. A deal with a bottom-level valuation of $500B might be worth more than $600B by the time it reaches the actual buyer.

Take SpaceX as an example. The actual market price is about $1.25T valuation + 3-11% access fee (different channels and different levels), which means the final price is about $1.375T, not including the compliance fees for tokenization. Overall, the price offered by the exchange is relatively fair, and it is likely for the purpose of attracting new users.

Moreover, most of the block supply in the market is fake—the same batch of shares is repeatedly listed by multiple brokers, with less than 10% actually available for trading. For example, SpaceX had a platform listing an estimated value of $1.2T, but after in-depth communication, it turned out to be all fake listings. Even large platforms and intermediaries are rife with this situation. If the transaction involves LP Interest Swap, you also need to obtain GP Consent, which is the GP of the underlying SPV's right to agree to the transfer of LP shares. The GP has the right to refuse. The reality in the industry is that GPs are not very welcoming of such transfers—because vetting new LPs, achieving compliance, and introducing strangers are all troublesome. Therefore, in many cases, it's necessary to bribe the GP to handle these matters, adding another layer of costs. Poor liquidity is the biggest pain point for pre-IPO existing shareholders. Selling midway is extremely difficult. Either you wait for the company's IPO (usually 3-7 years), and then there's generally a 6-month lock-up period after the IPO; or you find a buyer again and go through the structured process—two to three weeks (at the fastest) + FA fee. Each transfer is an independent OTC transaction, requiring redoing legal documents, KYC/AML/UBO due diligence, and GP approval. This is why pre-IPO companies are consistently priced as "illiquid assets." How can ordinary people participate in this round of Pre-IPO? It's foreseeable that a series of tokenized products based on existing shares will appear in the market. Essentially, they all work the same way: platforms purchase real existing shares in the traditional Pre-IPO market, then break them down into tokens and sell them to retail investors. For ordinary people, this provides an opportunity to enter the market before the company's IPO, allowing their valuation to rise naturally with each round. The valuations of top-tier, high-quality companies typically rise steadily. SpaceX's valuation has risen from $74 billion in 2021 to over $1.4 trillion now, OpenAI from $29 billion to over $852 billion, Anthropic from $4 billion to over $800 billion, and ByteDance from $75 billion to over $600 billion. Each new round of financing inflates valuations, and existing shareholders benefit accordingly. However, it's crucial to understand that this isn't a guaranteed profit. Historically, Stripe experienced a downturn, with its valuation halved from $95 billion to $50 billion; TrueLayer fell by over 30%, Cyberreason by over 90%, and WeWork went bankrupt from a valuation of $49 billion. In 2023, 128 unicorns globally saw their valuations decline, and 42 were removed from the unicorn list altogether.

Therefore, the key to participating in Pre-IPO is selecting targets, not betting on the timing, but rather following the natural rise in the company's valuation to earn long-term returns—not rushing to buy as soon as it launches and trying to profit from short-term fluctuations driven by sentiment. Many Crypto users treat Pre-IPO as akin to cryptocurrency IDOs, which are two completely different logics.

To summarize the participation logic:

First, do you have a long-term positive outlook on this target? Is SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic worth its post-IPO valuation?

... Are you willing to hold it until the next round of fundraising or IPO?

Second, is this product safe? Who is the issuer? Where is the safety net? Who can be held responsible if something goes wrong?

RWA Form in the Next 3 Years

Pre-IPO RWA is still in its very early stages. Leading companies have scarce supply, highly concentrated demand, and long-term upward valuations. In the next few months, tokenized products from leading companies such as OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Stripe, ByteDance, and Kimi will emerge one after another.

This is just a small branch of the entire tokenization process. Currently, the main four-layer structure is clearly foreseeable: Stablecoin issuers: providing on-chain USD and settlement access; Public blockchain networks: supporting asset issuance and circulation; Trading and distribution platforms: CEXs and DEXs. Additionally, we believe there is another potential player: LaunchPad/IDO platforms (such as Buidlpad), which already possess the full capabilities for new asset KYC, issuance, subscription, and distribution. In the past, they issued crypto tokens; today, they can easily issue Pre-IPO tokens. Asset issuance service providers: service companies that put various assets on-chain. It is foreseeable that the tokenization main line will not only give rise to a batch of unicorns, but also has the potential to nurture new trillion-dollar infrastructure and a group of hundred-billion-dollar platform players. This is just the beginning.

data.title: The "surge crash" signal has already appeared in the US stock market AI market.
data.description:
The higher the price goes, the more dangerous it becomes—this isn't just a pessimist's intuition, but a warning from Bank of America's quantitative model. The US stock market is currently in a rare situation: indices are hitting new highs, but volatility is also rising in tandem. This combination of "rising prices without decreasing volatility" is historically a typical characteristic of bubble phases. This week, the market will face two major tests: the FOMC interest rate decision and the earnings reports of five of the "Big Seven" tech companies. These two events combined create the most concentrated window of trading catalysts in recent times. "A Surge-Driven Crash": The Stronger the Rise, the Higher the Risk. According to Wind Trading, the bank's derivatives strategy team (analysts such as Nitin Saksena) made a startling assessment in their latest report: The Nasdaq has achieved a record 13-day winning streak, reaching a volatility level close to 25%—the S&P 500's gains are even comparable to those during the COVID-19 pandemic, but it did not experience the same level of market pressure beforehand. This 'surge-driven crash' dynamic is highly consistent with our prediction of a bubble market in 2026. What is a "rising crash"? Simply put, it's when prices surge while volatility rises instead of falling—the market is euphoric, but insurance premiums are also increasing. This usually indicates that market participants are not calm and their opinions on the direction are diverging. The AI ​​sector deserves even more attention. The report points out that the "bubble risk indicator" for AI-related assets such as semiconductors has risen to its highest level since the inception of ChatGPT. During last week's market surge, stock volatility not only did not decline but was actually supported—further evidence of bubble dynamics.

What will Powell say this time?

On Wednesday, April 29, the FOMC will announce its interest rate decision. Analysts write:

The Fed will firmly hold rates steady at its April meeting.

The Fed will firmly hold rates steady at its April meeting.

The upward risk of inflation from the Iran war has not yet dissipated, and labor market data has also improved. Powell is likely to release hawkish signals at the press conference. Three key points to watch: first, whether he is open to further interest rate hikes; second, how he assesses the impact of the war on the economy; and third, whether he will emphasize the recent recovery in the labor market. On the data front, analysts predict that the annualized GDP growth rate for the first quarter, to be released on April 30, will be 2.4% (market consensus is 1.6%), and the core PCE year-on-year growth rate is expected to be 3.1%. Inflation remains high, which is the fundamental reason why Powell is unlikely to turn dovish.

The earnings reports of the five major tech giants: all exceeded expectations

This week, the most attention-grabbing news in the market was the combined earnings reports of the five members of the "Big Seven" tech giants. Analysts' forecasts for all five companies were higher than the market consensus.

The most anticipated news this week was the combined earnings reports of the five members of the "Big Seven" tech giants. Analysts' forecasts for all five companies were higher than the market consensus. Meta (April 29) Analyst Justin Post projects first-quarter revenue/EPS of $56 billion/$7.44, higher than the market consensus of $55.4 billion/$6.64. The drivers are AI's empowerment of the core advertising business and continued cost control. The biggest near-term risk is macroeconomic uncertainty impacting second-quarter revenue guidance, or a further increase in AI infrastructure capital expenditure. Amazon (April 29) Justin Post projects first-quarter revenue/EBIT of $178.4 billion/$21.4 billion, higher than the market consensus of $177.1 billion/$20.7 billion. AWS growth forecast has been revised upward to 28% year-over-year, higher than the market consensus of 25%, partly driven by Anthropic-related revenue. Alphabet (April 29) Justin Post projects first-quarter revenue/EPS of $92 billion/$2.69, slightly higher than the market consensus of $91.7 billion/$2.66. Gemini model integration is expected to drive better-than-expected search and cloud business growth, with search revenue growth projected at 18%. Microsoft (April 29th) Analyst Tal Liani projects earnings per share of $4.05, slightly higher than the market consensus of $4.04. Key highlights include Azure revenue growth (as AI computing power is rolled out), the expansion of Copilot paid subscriptions, and the stability of non-AI businesses. Apple (April 30) Analyst Wamsi Mohan expects second-quarter revenue/earnings per share to be $113 billion/$2.00, higher than the market consensus of $109 billion/$1.93, mainly supported by strong iPhone sales.

data.title: Arthur Hayes: Bitcoin's year-end price target is $125,000
data.description:
Video Source: Bitcoin Magazine   Compiled by: Felix, PANews

BitMEX co-founder Arthur Hayes delivered a speech at the Bitcoin 2026 conference. In this speech, Hayes explained why he is bullish on Bitcoin, why Kevin Warsh is not the hawkish figure people fear, and how a banking regulatory rule that quietly took effect on April 1 could unleash trillions of dollars in new credit. Furthermore, Hayes proposed a year-end target price of $125,000 for Bitcoin and explained his "wartime money printing" theory that led to this target.

PANews has compiled the following details of the speech.

Over the past few days, I have deeply considered how money printing policies will evolve, taking into account the development of AI and the situation of the Iran war, which led to the content of this speech.

Obviously, my stance has shifted to a more bullish one, and I will explain why below. Of course, we cannot ignore the ongoing war, so before elaborating on my core arguments, I must establish a few assumptions. First, we will not die from nuclear annihilation; because once nuclear annihilation occurs, any investment becomes meaningless, so let's set that concern aside for now. Second, the market will view this event as some kind of "short-term" event, whatever that means. Now is the time to think about money creation and printing, and what that means for Bitcoin. Every morning I analyze the actual impact of the war on my portfolio using a chart from Bloomberg. This chart shows the price difference between the six-month WTI crude oil futures contract and the current month contract. I don't care at all about the propaganda war from Trump or Iran; my only concern is: are there enough commodities and oil passing through the Straits smoothly? The chart shows that the situation has improved, meaning that front-end prices are trending towards the back-end, indicating that while the situation is bad, it's not at its worst. So I can temporarily ignore it and continue thinking about other things.

Every time I give a speech, I always talk about printing money. However, my thinking has shifted since my last article about two weeks ago. I now believe that in the medium to long term, liquidity will turn positive. Therefore, if we consider the negative side, we see deflation caused by AI. There has been much discussion about how many knowledge workers will lose their jobs because efficient and inexpensive models can perform knowledge-based work. A few months ago, I wrote an article outlining my expectations for these losses. I believe this could result in hundreds of billions of dollars in losses for the banking system.

As for the Federal Reserve, I'll discuss that later. The market is very concerned about the nominee for Fed Chair, Kevin Worsh, with everyone speculating whether he will be a hawk or a dove. I will analyze his remarks objectively; basically, his comments are neutral, neither beneficial nor harmful to liquidity.

Those market participants who panicked about Warsh being a super-hawkish Fed chairman actually missed the underlying signal. Finally, let's look at commercial bank lending. Why is commercial bank lending increasing? Why would the war economy in the US and overseas prompt banks to extend more loans to those involved in the production of various weapons and related components?
