So, putting AI aside for now, let's talk about the Federal Reserve. In January of this year, when Kevin Warsh was nominated as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, the market panicked. Since the 2008 financial crisis, he has been critical of the Fed's massive balance sheet and has publicly stated his intention to shrink the balance sheet and cut interest rates. If you've read my articles, you know that I've always argued that the quantity of money is more important than its price. So I'm more concerned about his comments on the balance sheet than about where short-term interest rates will go. If the market believes that dollar liquidity will decrease because of Warsh's appointment, then they will be bearish on Bitcoin and other risk assets. That's what we've been seeing in the media lately as the "soon-to-be super-hawkish Fed chair." These regulations will restrict how banks hold assets on their balance sheets and the capital they must hold for that. But I don't think that will happen. I think the Fed will essentially be transferring reserves, Treasury bonds, and repurchase agreements to the commercial banking system, and doing so through new banking regulations. These regulations will restrict how banks hold assets on their balance sheets and the capital they must hold for that. Finally, I think the most important thing to understand Warren's impact on the Fed is to understand that he faces a very important constraint: he must work closely with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant to ensure that any actions he takes on the Fed's balance sheet do not impair Bessant's ability to issue billions of dollars in bonds. Here's a very simple balance sheet. There aren't specific numbers here because I know it's a bit complicated for some people. On the asset side, there are Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and repurchase agreements. These are tools that help people finance the purchase of Treasury bonds. On the liability side, there are bank reserves, the Treasury's general account, government checking accounts, and currency in circulation. Basically, since 2008, the Federal Reserve has increased its liabilities on the bank reserve side and purchased assets from the banking system. These assets include Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and repurchase agreements. When Warren said the balance sheet was too large, he meant that the Federal Reserve held too many bonds and he wanted to shrink the balance sheet. So, he might sell bonds. But that would have a huge impact on the market. Or, I think what's being implied right now is that he will engage in asset swaps with the U.S. banking system. The balance sheets of commercial banks, that is, the Federal Reserve's reserves, are also considered assets. There are approximately three trillion dollars in reserves held on the Federal Reserve's balance sheet. Their funding sources include loans, deposits, and shareholder equity. Therefore, for a given balance sheet size, there must be a corresponding amount of equity. This is known as the capital adequacy ratio. So, the Federal Reserve and banks need to engage in a swap. Banks need to release reserves, reduce their demand for reserves, and replace these reserves with Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements. This is precisely what the deregulation of the US commercial banking system has driven. So, whenever you hear US government monetary officials talk about deregulation, they mean that we want to allow the banking system to absorb all the debt we create and remove it from the Federal Reserve's balance sheet. The ultimate goal is for US commercial banks to take over the baton of money creation from the Federal Reserve, with their balance sheets containing Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements, and their liabilities consisting of deposits and shareholder equity. The key to all this is that the net impact on dollar liquidity is neutral. Nothing is sold, and nothing is bought; it's simply a swap transaction. As far as who can hold what is concerned, this is purely a regulatory expedient. Ultimately, Warsh can step up and tell everyone he successfully shrank the Fed's balance sheet. But in reality, as investors, we only care about the end result? The end result is nothing. Furthermore, Warsh won't clash with Bessant. They can simply replace Powell's face in their previous photo with Warsh's. Ultimately, we've issued $38 trillion in debt, and the government needs the funds. The Fed will fulfill its duty to ensure orderly markets so that people can buy these debts. Let's look at the spending situation; this is a chart for the current fiscal year, from October to September of the following year. As we can see, from the COVID-19 pandemic to the presidency, and now to the largest peacetime deficit in US history, the deficit in fiscal year 2026 is slightly higher than in fiscal year 2025. Now, the key point of all this is that the US Treasury is not reducing spending. Trump has not said he will drastically cut spending. Last year's DOGE plan has been forgotten. Now it's all wartime spending. His new defense budget is 50% higher than before, reaching $1.5 trillion. This doesn't sound like the Treasury or politicians are going to cut spending together so the Federal Reserve can shrink its balance sheet. Therefore, all the talk about the Federal Reserve shrinking its balance sheet doesn't make sense, because politicians and the Treasury behind them are constantly increasing the debt. Here's another chart. Who is buying this debt? Foreigners are buying less debt than before. I've excluded countries that are typically used for hedge fund basis trading. As we can see, the 25% share of debt held by foreigners has remained largely unchanged, while the total debt has increased significantly. This means a new, price-insensitive buyer is needed to acquire all this debt, and that buyer is the US commercial banking system. The reason the banking system can increase its debt holdings is due to the new regulatory rule that took effect on April 1st of this year: the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio (ESLR). This new rule allows banks to hold fewer reserves and other types of assets to support loans and other items on their balance sheets. This means that large banks like JPMorgan Chase and Citigroup can issue more Treasury bonds and repurchase bonds in the market and obtain rollovers from the Federal Reserve. For smaller banks, which are the engine of lending in the US economy, they can increase the amount of construction and industrial loans they issue. S&P Global estimates that this ESLR and balance sheet reduction will generate $1.3 trillion in new loans. So, why do banks have a demand for loans? One criticism of this analysis from some other macroeconomic analysts is that they argue the banking system lacks demand, failing to create enough loans, or that demand is insufficient. But there is an excellent source of demand: the U.S. Department of Defense. They not only put equity in certain deals but also provide guarantees for underwriting production. Banks see companies with guaranteed clients like the government (which can print money) and lend to them. They also lend to resource extractors who mine the critical resources needed to make bombs. Finally, all AI capital expenditures are now considered national security issues. Therefore, when a mega-corporation cannot finance its debt with free cash flow and turns to market financing, they find large banks with massive balance sheets willing to provide debt support.
